audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman calling a tourist information office to ask about a festival.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, you're through to the tourist information office.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Tim speaking.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Could you give me some information about next month's festival, please?
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: My family and I will be staying in the town that week.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Well, it starts with a concert on the afternoon of the 17th.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Oh, I heard about that.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: The orchestra and singers come from the USA, don't they?
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: They're from Canada.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: They're very popular over there.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: They're going to perform a number of well-known pieces that will appeal to children as well as adults.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: That sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: My whole family are interested in music.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: The next day, the 18th, there's a performance by a ballet company called Eustatis.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Sorry?
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: The name is spelled EUSTATIS.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: They appeared in last year's festival and went down very well.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Again, their program is designed for all ages.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Good, I expect we'll go to that.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: I hope there's going to be a play during the festival.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: A comedy ideally.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: You're in luck.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: On the 19th and 20th, a local amateur group are performing one written by a member of the group.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: It's called Jemima.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: That'll be on in the town hall.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: They've already performed it 2 or 3 times.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I haven't seen it myself, but the review in the local paper was very good.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: And is it suitable for children?
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Yes, in fact, it's aimed more at children than at adults, so both performances are in the afternoon.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: And what about dance?
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Will there be any performances?
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Yes, also on the 20th, but in the evening.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: A professional company is putting on a show of modern pieces.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: With electronic music by young composers.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: The show is about how people communicate or fail to communicate with each other, so it's got the rather strange name.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Chat.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I suppose that's because that's something we do both face to face and online.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, there are also some workshops and other activities.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: They'll all take place at least once every day, so everyone who wants to take part will have a chance.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Good, we're particularly interested in cookery.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: You don't happen to have a cookery workshop, do you?
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: We certainly do.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: It's going to focus on how to make food part of a healthy lifestyle.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: And it'll show that even sweet things like cakes can contain much less sugar than they usually do.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Hmm. That might be worth going to.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: We're trying to encourage our children to cook.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Another workshop is just for children, and that's on creating posters.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: To reflect the history of the town.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: The aim is to make children aware of how both the town and people's lives have changed over the centuries.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: The results will be exhibited in the community center.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Then, the other workshop is in toy making, and that's for adults only.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Why is that?
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Because it involves carpentry.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Participants will be making toys out of wood, so there'll be a lot of sharp chisels and other tools around.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: It makes sense to keep children away from it.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Now let me tell you about some of the outdoor activities.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Uh. There'll be supervised wild swimming.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Wild swimming.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: What's that?
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: It just means swimming in natural waters, rather than a swimming pool.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Oh, OK.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: In a lake, for instance?
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: There's a beautiful one just outside the town, and that'll be the venue for the swimming.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: There'll be lifeguards on duty, so it's suitable for all ages.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: And finally, there'll be a walk in some nearby woods every day.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: The leader is an expert on insects.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: He'll show some that live in the woods, and how important they are for the environment.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: So, there are going to be all sorts of different things to do during the festival.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: There certainly are.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: If you'd like to read about how the preparations for the festival are going.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: The festival organizer is keeping a blog.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Just search online for the festival website, and you'll find it.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Well, thank you very much for all the information.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: You're welcome.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: JEAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a talk to a gardening club about a local park.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I'm very pleased to welcome this evening's guest speaker, Mark Logan, who's going to tell us about the recent transformation of Minster Park.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Over to you, Mark.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: I'm sure you're all familiar with Minster Park.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: It's been a feature of the city for well over a century, and has been the responsibility of the city council for most of that time.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: What perhaps isn't so well known is the origin of the park.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Unlike many public parks that started in private ownership, as the garden of a large house for instance, Minster was some waste land, which people living nearby started planting with flowers in 1892.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: It was unclear who actually owned the land.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: And this wasn't settled until 20 years later, when the council took possession of it.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: You may have noticed the statue near one of the entrances.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: It's of Diane Gosforth, who played a key role in the history of the park.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Once the council had become the legal owner, it planned to sell the land for housing.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Many local people wanted it to remain a place that everyone could go to, to enjoy the fresh air and natural environment.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Remember, the park is in a densely populated residential area.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Diane Gosforth was one of those people, and she organized petitions and demonstrations, which eventually made the council change its mind about the future of the land.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Soon after this, the First World War broke out, in 1914,
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: and most of the park was dug up and planted with vegetables,
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: which were sold locally, at one stage, the army considered taking it over for troop exercises, and got as far as contacting the city council, then decided the park was too small to be of use.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: There were occasional public meetings during the war, in an area that had been retained as grass.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: After the war, the park was turned back more or less to how it had been before 1914 and continued almost unchanged until recently.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Plans for transforming it were drawn up at various times, most recently in 2013, though they were revised in 2015 before any work had started.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: The changes finally got going in 2016 and were finished on schedule last year.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, let me tell you about some of the changes that have been made, and some things that have been retained.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: If you look at this map.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: You'll see the familiar outline of the park, with the river forming the northern boundary, and a gate in each of the other three walls.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: The statue of Diane Gosforth has been moved.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: It used to be close to the south gate, but it's now immediately to the north of the lily pond, almost in the center of the park, which makes it much more visible.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: There's a new area of wooden sculptures, which are on the river bank, where the path from the east gate makes a sharp bend.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: There are two areas that are particularly intended for children.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: The playground has been enlarged and improved, and that's between the river and the path that leads from the pond to the river.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Then there's a new maze, a circular series of paths separated by low hedges.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: That's near the west gate.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: You go north from there towards the river.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: And then turn left to reach it.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: There have been tennis courts in the park for many years, and they've been doubled from four to eight.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: They're still in the south-west corner of the park.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Where there's a right-angle bend in the path.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Uh. Something else I'd like to mention is the new fitness area.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: This is right next to the lily pond, on the same side as the west gate.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: Now, as you're all gardeners, I'm sure you'll like to hear about the plants that have been chosen for the park.
audioscript.speaker: MARK

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: The playground has been enlarged and improved, and that's between the river and the path that leads from the pond to the river.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: Then there's a new maze, a circular series of paths separated by low hedges.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: That's near the west gate.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: You go north from there towards the river.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: And then turn left to reach it.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: There have been tennis courts in the park for many years, and they've been doubled from four to eight.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: They're still in the south-west corner of the park.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: Where there's a right-angle bend in the path.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: Uh. Something else I'd like to mention is the new fitness area.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: This is right next to the lily pond, on the same side as the west gate.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: Now, as you're all gardeners, I'm sure you'll like to hear about the plants that have been chosen for the park.
utterances.speaker: MARK

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: The park was originally established
options: as an amenity provided by the city council.
as land belonging to a private house.
as a shared area set up by the local community.

items.title: Why is there a statue of Diane Gosforth in the park?
options: She was a resident who helped to lead a campaign.
She was a council member responsible for giving the public access.
She was a senior worker at the park for many years.

items.title: During the First World War, the park was mainly used for
When did the physical transformation of the park begin?

audioscript.text: Part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two students of English called Cathy and Graham planning a display on 19th century British life and literature for a university event.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, Graham, so let's check we both know what we're supposed to be doing.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: So for the university's open day, we have to plan a display on British life and literature in the mid 19th century.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: But we'll have some people to help us find the materials and set it up, remember?
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: For the moment, we just need to plan it.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: So have you gathered who's expected to come and see the display?
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Is it for the people studying English or students from other departments?
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: I'm not clear about it.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Nor me.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: That was how it used to be, but it didn't attract many people so this year it's going to be part of an open day to raise the university's profile.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: It'll be publicized in the city to encourage people to come and find out something of what goes on here.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: And it's included in the information that's sent to people who are considering applying to study here next year.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Presumably some current students and lecturers will come.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: I would imagine so.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: But we've been told to concentrate on the other categories of people.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: We don't have to cover the whole range of 19th century literature, do we?
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: No, it's entirely up to us.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: I suggest just using Charles Dickens.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: That's a good idea.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Most people have heard of him and have probably read some of his novels or seen films based on them.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: So that's a good lead-in to life in his time.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: And his novels show the awful conditions that most people had to live in, don't they?
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: He wanted to shock people into doing something about it.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Did he do any campaigning other than writing?
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Yes, he campaigned for education and other social reforms and gave talks, but I'm inclined to ignore that and focus on the novels.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Yes, I agree.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, so now should we think about a topic linked to each novel?
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Yes, I've printed out a list of Dickens's novels in the order they were published.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: In the hope you'd agree to focus on him.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Ha ha ha, you're lucky.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: I did agree.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Ha ha.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Let's have a look.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Uh. OK, the first was the Pickwick Papers.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Published in 1836 it was very successful when it came out, wasn't it?
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: And was adapted for the theatre straight away.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: There's an interesting point though.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: That there's a character who keeps falling asleep, and that medical condition was named after the book, Pickwickian syndrome.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Oh, so why don't we use that as the topic?
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: And include some quotations from the novel.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Right, next is Oliver Twist.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: There's a lot in the novel about poverty, but maybe something less obvious.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Well, Oliver is taught how to steal, isn't he?
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: We could use that to illustrate the fact that very few children went to school, particularly not poor children, so they learned in other ways.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: What's next?
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Maybe Nicholas Nickleby.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Actually he taught in a really cruel school, didn't he?
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: But there's also the company of touring actors that Nicholas joins.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: We could do something on theaters and other amusements of the time.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: We don't want only the bad things, do we?
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: OK, what about Martin Chuzzlewit?
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: He goes to the USA, doesn't he?
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Yes, and Dickens himself had been there a year before.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: And drew on his experience there in the novel.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: I wonder though, the main theme is selfishness, so we could do something on social justice.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Hmm. No, too general.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Let's keep to your idea.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: I think it would work well.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: He wrote Bleak House next, that's my favorite of his novels.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Yes, mine too.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Oh, his satire of the legal system is pretty powerful.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: That's true, but think about Esther the heroine.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: As a child, she lives with someone she doesn't know is her aunt, who treats her very badly.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Then she's very happy living with her guardian, and he puts her in charge of the household, and at the end she gets married, and her guardian gives her and her husband a house, where of course they're very happy.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Yes, I like that.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: What should we take next?
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Uh. Little Dorrit, old Mr Dorrit has been in a debtor's prison for years.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: So was Dickens's father, wasn't he?
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: What about focusing on the part when Mr Dorrit inherits a fortune?
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: And he starts pretending he's always been rich.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Ha ha, good idea.
audioscript.speaker: CATHY

audioscript.text: Ha ha, OK.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: So next we need to think about what materials we want to illustrate each issue.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: That's going to be quite hard.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO groups of people is the display primarily intended for?
What are Cathy and Graham's TWO reasons for choosing the novelist Charles Dickens?
options: His speeches inspired others to try to improve society.
He used his publications to draw attention to social problems.
He was consulted on a number of social issues.
His reputation has changed in recent times.

text: What topic do Cathy and Graham choose to illustrate with each novel?
textReadable: What topic do Cathy and Graham choose to illustrate with each novel?

audioscript.text: Part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a presentation about an agricultural program in the country of Mozambique.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I'm going to report on a case study of a program which has been set up to help rural populations in Mozambique, a largely agricultural country in Southeast Africa.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The program worked with three communities in Chicualacuala district near the Limpopo River.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is a dry and arid region with unpredictable rainfall.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because of this, people in the area were unable to support themselves through agriculture, and instead they used the forest,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: as a means of providing themselves with an income, mainly by selling charcoal.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, this was not a sustainable way of living in the long term, as they were rapidly using up this resource.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To support agriculture in this dry region, the program focused primarily on making use of existing water resources from the Limpopo River,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: by setting up systems of irrigation, which would provide a dependable water supply for crops and animals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The program worked closely with the district government in order to find the best way of implementing this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The region already had one farmers' association, and it was decided to set up two more of these.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These associations planned and carried out activities, including water management, livestock breeding and agriculture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it was notable that in general, women formed the majority of the workforce.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was decided that in order to keep the crops safe from animals, both wild and domestic, special areas should be fenced off.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Where the crops could be grown.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The community was responsible for creating these fences, but the program provided the necessary wire for making them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Once the area had been fenced off, it could be cultivated.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The land was dug, so that vegetables and cereals appropriate to the climate could be grown, and the program provided the necessary seeds for this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The program also provided pumps, so that water could be brought from the river in pipes to the fields.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, the labor was all provided by local people, and they also provided and put up the posts that supported the fences around the fields.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Once the program had been set up, its development was monitored carefully.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The farmers were able to grow enough produce, not just for their own needs,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: but also to sell.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, getting the produce to places where it could be marketed was sometimes a problem, as the farmers did not have access to transport.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this resulted in large amounts of produce, especially vegetables, being spoiled.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This problem was discussed with the farmers' associations, and it was decided that in order to prevent food from being spoiled.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The farmers needed to learn techniques for its preservation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There was also an additional initiative that had not been originally planned, but which became a central feature of the program.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was when farmers started to dig holes for tanks in the fenced-off areas, and to fill these with water, and use them for breeding fish, an important source of protein.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After a time, another suggestion was made by local people, which hadn't been part of the program's original proposal.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But which was also adopted later on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They decided to try setting up colonies of bees, which would provide honey, both for their own consumption and to sell.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what lessons can be learned from this program?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First of all, it tells us that in dry, arid regions, if there is access to a reliable source of water,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: there is great potential for the development of agriculture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Chicualacuala, there was a marked improvement in agricultural production,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: which improved food security and benefited local people by providing them with both food and income.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, it's important to set realistic timelines for each phase of the program, especially for its design.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As mistakes made at this stage may be hard to correct later on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The program demonstrates that sustainable development is possible in areas where there is...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a presentation about an agricultural program in the country of Mozambique.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: I'm going to report on a case study of a program which has been set up to help rural populations in Mozambique, a largely agricultural country in Southeast Africa.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The program worked with three communities in Chicualacuala district near the Limpopo River.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is a dry and arid region with unpredictable rainfall.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because of this, people in the area were unable to support themselves through agriculture, and instead they used the forest,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: as a means of providing themselves with an income, mainly by selling charcoal.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, this was not a sustainable way of living in the long term, as they were rapidly using up this resource.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To support agriculture in this dry region, the program focused primarily on making use of existing water resources from the Limpopo River,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: by setting up systems of irrigation, which would provide a dependable water supply for crops and animals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The program worked closely with the district government in order to find the best way of implementing this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The region already had one farmers' association, and it was decided to set up two more of these.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These associations planned and carried out activities, including water management, livestock breeding and agriculture.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And it was notable that in general, women formed the majority of the workforce.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was decided that in order to keep the crops safe from animals, both wild and domestic, special areas should be fenced off.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Where the crops could be grown.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The community was responsible for creating these fences, but the program provided the necessary wire for making them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Once the area had been fenced off, it could be cultivated.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The land was dug, so that vegetables and cereals appropriate to the climate could be grown, and the program provided the necessary seeds for this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The program also provided pumps, so that water could be brought from the river in pipes to the fields.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, the labor was all provided by local people, and they also provided and put up the posts that supported the fences around the fields.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Once the program had been set up, its development was monitored carefully.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The farmers were able to grow enough produce, not just for their own needs,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: but also to sell.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, getting the produce to places where it could be marketed was sometimes a problem, as the farmers did not have access to transport.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And this resulted in large amounts of produce, especially vegetables, being spoiled.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This problem was discussed with the farmers' associations, and it was decided that in order to prevent food from being spoiled.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The farmers needed to learn techniques for its preservation.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There was also an additional initiative that had not been originally planned, but which became a central feature of the program.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This was when farmers started to dig holes for tanks in the fenced-off areas, and to fill these with water, and use them for breeding fish, an important source of protein.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After a time, another suggestion was made by local people, which hadn't been part of the program's original proposal.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But which was also adopted later on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They decided to try setting up colonies of bees, which would provide honey, both for their own consumption and to sell.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So what lessons can be learned from this program?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: First of all, it tells us that in dry, arid regions, if there is access to a reliable source of water,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: there is great potential for the development of agriculture.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In Chicualacuala, there was a marked improvement in agricultural production,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: which improved food security and benefited local people by providing them with both food and income.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, it's important to set realistic timelines for each phase of the program, especially for its design.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As mistakes made at this stage may be hard to correct later on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The program demonstrates that sustainable development is possible in areas where there is...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
